1: <header>Carbon footprint of raw milk for direct human consumption produced in Po Valley: Data input uncertainties and effect on output variance </header>
Federico Froldia, Lucrezia Lamastra b Sara Corradoc, Maurizio Moschini a,*

<list>a Department of Animal Science, Food and Nutrition (DIANA), Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore, via Emilia Parmense 84, Piacenza 29122, Italy 
b Department for Sustainable Food Process (DiSTAS), Università Cattolica del Sacro Cuore, via Emilia Parmense 84, Piacenza 29122, Italy 
C Resource Valorization Laboratory, Department for Sustainability, ENEA (Italian National Agency for New Technologies, Energy and Sustainable Economic Development), Via Anguillarese 301, Rome, Italy</list>

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Life cycle assessment Greenhouse gasses Dairy farm Feed purchase Monte Carlo simulation|A survey on 61 dairy farms in the Po Valley was carried out using an LCA approach to identify milk production's carbon footprint (CF) at the farm gate, with 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) as a functional unit. Data were collected and grouped into five main categories: feed purchase, in farm feed production, energy & resources, enteric fermentation, and livestock manure. The CF calculation was conducted according to IPCC guidelines 2006. Dairy herds were classified into three groups according to quartiles for the number of lactating dairy cows: small (< 87), medium (between 87 and 270), and large herds (
2: The growing population challenges agriculture and livestock breed- ing to improve their production by taking the path of sustainability. Improving the efficiency and the sustainability of production systems are critical factors in dealing with increasing competition for natural resources, the effects of climate change, the reduction of biodiversity and soil fertility (United Nations, 2019). The latest assessment report of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2014) iden- tifies anthropogenic greenhouse gasses (GHG) emissions at high lev- els (Porter et al., 2014). Agriculture and food systems are significant sources of GHG and, at the same time, particularly exposed to climate changes (FAO, 2018). GHG has increased significantly in developing and developed countries and is expected to increase further in several decades (Tubiello et al., 2015). The GHG are emitted by fuel combus- tion, deforestation, enteric fermentation, manure management, produc- tion and use of nitrogen fertilizers (Pirlo and Lolli, 2019). The fertilizers and pesticides used for plant growth have drawbacks on the ecosys- tems (She et al., 2017). Animal rearing affects climate change with se-

vere impacts on air, water, and soil quality (Egas et al., 2020; Rotz and Thoma, 2017). Gerber (2013) estimates emissions from the livestock sector at 7.1 gigatons of CO2 equivalent (CO-eq) per year, accounting for 14.5% of global anthropogenic GHG emissions. The main contribu- tions are from feed production and processing and enteric fermentation from ruminants (Ammar et al., 2020), representing 45 and 39% of the sector emissions. In contrast, the manure storage and processing all to- gether settle to 10%. The remaining part of the emissions is related to transport and farming processes. However, the trend is for an increase of methane (CH4) (+21%; Smith et al., 2007) and nitrous oxide (N20) (+35%; Nielsen et al., 2011) emissions by 2030. The contribution of food production to climate change can be assessed considering the GHG emis- sions through the life cycle assessment (LCA) of products (Goglio et al., 2020), and the adoption of this approach in recent years suggests an increasing focus on sustainable production systems on a global scale (Crenna et al., 2019). Then, the LCA approach was adapted and inte- grated to assess the environmental impacts of agricultural processes. This objective assessment systematically considers the quantification of the energy and environmental loads related to a process or activity by significant inputs and outputs associated with a specific reference unit of a product system in a defined system boundary (Hauschild et al., 2018). The system boundary and the functional unit (FU) are defined within the research goal. The assessment quantifies resources used and emissions released from them into the environment in all the consid-

ered phases (Mehmeti et al., 2018), enables sustainable development (Arfini et al., 2019), and it is considered valuable for estimating the impact of dairy production on climate change (Carvalho et al., 2021; Herrero et al., 2015). However, CH4 and N2O as the main contribu- tors to GHG from animal breeding (Knapp et al., 2014) because re- lated to complex biological processes about GHG emission, carries in- herent variabilities leading to significant uncertainties not constant in the assessment (Charkovska et al., 2018). Moreover, because of how GHG emissions can be calculated and the adopted assumption when performing the carbon footprint (CF), a comparison between similar studies is not appropriate (Basset-Mens, 2008) and could lead to an in- conclusive deduction. Thus, when estimating the CF for FU, the chosen system boundary is a methodological aspect as well as co-products han- dling or methods for valuing feed components. The latter, along with the model used for calculating the CH4 emission from enteric fermen- tation, are sources of uncertainties in CF estimates and emission fac- tors from manure and land after manure distribution. The use of In- ternational Guidelines such as IPCC (2006) to estimate emissions of CH4 from enteric fermentation in livestock and CH4 and N2O emissions from manure management could be proper support and methodologi- cal reference to be considered in LCA studies of the livestock sector. Similarly, the International Dairy Federation (IDF, 2015), which allows for a clear definition of the weights assigned to the main product and co-products regarding farm boundaries, is a common approach for CF allocation.

Uncertainty and variability analyses in LCA studies have been conducted to increase awareness of the importance and impact of this topic in LCA practitioners (Michiels and Geeraerd, 2020). Huijbregts (1998) defined three types of uncertainty: parameter uncer- tainty, model uncertainty, and uncertainty due to choices. Thus, uncer- tainty scenarios could result from the choice of FU and system bound- aries in the goal and scope definition phase, the choice of the allocation procedures in the inventory phase, the choice for a particular time hori- zon in the impact assessment phase (Huijbregts et al., 2003). Usually, uncertainty analysis in the LCA study is carried out by a Monte Carlo (MC) approach implemented in the SimaPro software (PRé Consultants, Swiss centre for Life Cycle Inventories). However, practitioners' choices can also affect reproducibility when repeatability is guaranteed by se- lecting high-quality data. This work proposes an approach to conducting a sensitivity analysis in the case of LCA analysis of raw milk production in farms located in Northern Italy, specifically in the Po Valley. The impact of input data uncertainties is established on the output variance. The approach includes quantifying data input uncertainties regarding their probabil- ity distribution functions, propagation through a MC simulation, then a sensitivity analysis through variance decomposition to point out key parameters uncertainties mostly affecting LCA results. Then, based on the input variance effect on the variance of impact indicator, effec- tive mitigation strategies can be planned accordingly to the original dataset.

6: According to ISO standards 14040 and 14044 (ISO 2006a, 2006b), an LCA study consists of four distinct phases: goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment, and interpretation of the results.

The study aimed to perform CF of milk produced for direct human consumption. Primary data were collected in the year 2016 over 61 dairy farms located in Northern Italy in the province of Cremona, Man- tua, and Lodi, and referred to a whole calendar year (2015). The farms' survey was carried out to estimate the CF of selected farms and to plan mitigation actions to improve their sustainability through the selection of appropriate and effective mitigation practices.
7: The FU was 1 kg of fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) at the farm gate (cradle-to-farm). The FPCM (kg/y) was obtained considering a milk fat and true protein contents of 4% and 3.3%, respectively (IDF, 2015) using the following equation (Eq. (1)): FPCM (kg/y) = raw milk (kg/y) X (0.1226 fat content % + 0.0776 true protein % + 0.2534) (1)

The allocation between milk and co-products (Table 1) was per- formed following the ISO standard (ISO, 2006). The analyzed system has two outcomes: milk and meat. The latter is a co-product and refers to culled cows and male or extra female calves, either sold just after birth or after being reared on the farm. Thus, the allocation was based on physical relationships between the product (milk) and co-products, whereas the allocation of impacts to breeding co-products was according to IDF, 2015.

The dynamic of herd size expansion or restriction within the survey year could bias the allocation of CF. Thus, the proportion of lactating dairy cows over the whole herd and the replacement rate were used to exclude nine of the surveyed farms (Table 1).

No allocation was considered for livestock's manure exiting the sys- tem when transferred to other farms without any remuneration (eco- nomic allocation) as well as for feed sold (Fig. 1), i.e., cereals and hay. Also, inputs needed for their cultivation (diesel, synthetic fertilizers, and pesticides) were not considered but subtracted from the total inputs for self-produced feeds.

9: <header>2.3. Description of the dairy production system and inventory data collection </header>

The surveyed farms were classified according to quartiles for the number of lactating dairy cows. Three groups were obtained: small herds (number of lactating dairy cows lower than the first quartile; < 87), medium herds (number of lactating dairy cows in between first and third quartile, 87 and 270), large herds (number of lactating dairy cows higher than the third quartile, > 270).

Table 2 reports averages of inventory data collected in dairy farms. Creating the life cycle inventory (LCI) allows understanding the input and output flow materials and the use of resources and energy within the system under study (Crawford et al., 2018). Animal breeding contributes to the emissions of CH4, N2O, and CO2 (Ammar et al., 2020) that can be ranked in primary (Finnegan et al., 2017) and secondary sources of emissions (Wilkes et al., 2020; Rotz et al., 2010). Primary emissions refer to the GHG emitted by the milk production phase, while secondary emissions are from sectors producing products and used by the system (i.e., dairy farming).

Information was grouped by feed purchase, in farm feed production, and energy & resources used and were reported as average for all herds and obtained groups based on the size of the lactating dairy cows herd. Data were collected through a checklist and referred to the following macro-areas:

<list>Breeding: number of animals and characteristics of the different cat- legories, purchase or sale of animals, performance data, phenotyping data; 
Milk production: milk yield and quality (fat and protein contents); 
Soil management for in farm crop production: cultivation, land and crop management, inputs used such as fertilizers, pesticides, and fuel; 
Feeds used in animal feeding: type, nutrients composition, origin (purchased or in farm produced);</list>

<list>- Energy & resources: type of energy, fuels, type of bedding materials and sanitizers; 
- Manure management: type of manure and/or slurry production, stor- age and system used for on-field distribution.</list>
Collected primary data were validated through consultation with the farmer manager and supported with additional information collected and related to breeding activities such as:

<list>- Planned manure agronomic use; 
- Fertilizers and pesticides register; 
- Amount of gasoline used (from the agricultural fuel register); 
- Energy consumption; 
- Collective synthetic breed (i.e., functional herd data); 
- Amount of feed purchase; 
- Milk composition; 
- Composition of diets fed to calves, young heifers, heifers, lactating cows, dry cows.</list>

To simplify data collection and estimate of the CF, animals were classified according to IPCC (2006) guidelines: calves (from birth to weaning); young heifers (from 2 to 12 months of age); heifers (from 12 months of age until first calving); lactating cows; dry cows (non- lactating pregnant cows).

Emissions were estimated according to the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). A miscellaneous Tier approach was used for emission estimates: Tier 2 for the CH4 emissions from animal enteric fermentation and ma- nure handling, and Tier 1 for the direct and indirect N20 emissions (Table 3).

Primary and secondary contributions to GHG emissions for feed pur- chase, in farm feed production, energy & resources (other purchases), enteric fermentation, and manure handling are depicted in Fig. 1.

The CH4 emission from manure was calculated as a percentage of the excreted volatile solids (IPCC, 2006b). The estimate of direct N2O emission considered the amount of manure/slurry produced (m ³/year)

10: <header>2.3. Description of the dairy production system and inventory data collection </header>

The surveyed farms were classified according to quartiles for the number of lactating dairy cows. Three groups were obtained: small herds (number of lactating dairy cows lower than the first quartile; < 87), medium herds (number of lactating dairy cows in between first and third quartile, 87 and 270), large herds (number of lactating dairy cows higher than the third quartile, > 270).

Table 2 reports averages of inventory data collected in dairy farms. Creating the life cycle inventory (LCI) allows understanding the input and output flow materials and the use of resources and energy within the system under study (Crawford et al., 2018). Animal breeding contributes to the emissions of CH4, N2O, and CO2 (Ammar et al., 2020) that can be ranked in primary (Finnegan et al., 2017) and secondary sources of emissions (Wilkes et al., 2020; Rotz et al., 2010). Primary emissions refer to the GHG emitted by the milk production phase, while secondary emissions are from sectors producing products and used by the system (i.e., dairy farming).

Information was grouped by feed purchase, in farm feed production, and energy & resources used and were reported as average for all herds and obtained groups based on the size of the lactating dairy cows herd. Data were collected through a checklist and referred to the following macro-areas:

<list>Breeding: number of animals and characteristics of the different cat- legories, purchase or sale of animals, performance data, phenotyping data; 
Milk production: milk yield and quality (fat and protein contents); 
Soil management for in farm crop production: cultivation, land and crop management, inputs used such as fertilizers, pesticides, and fuel; 
Feeds used in animal feeding: type, nutrients composition, origin (purchased or in farm produced);</list>

Fig. 1. System boundary, input and output considered in the LCA study in the description of the dairy farm system.

<list>- Energy & resources: type of energy, fuels, type of bedding materials and sanitizers; 
- Manure management: type of manure and/or slurry production, stor- age and system used for on-field distribution.</list>
Collected primary data were validated through consultation with the farmer manager and supported with additional information collected and related to breeding activities such as:

<list>- Planned manure agronomic use; 
- Fertilizers and pesticides register; 
- Amount of gasoline used (from the agricultural fuel register); 
- Energy consumption; 
- Collective synthetic breed (i.e., functional herd data); 
- Amount of feed purchase; 
- Milk composition; 
- Composition of diets fed to calves, young heifers, heifers, lactating cows, dry cows.</list>

To simplify data collection and estimate of the CF, animals were classified according to IPCC (2006) guidelines: calves (from birth to weaning); young heifers (from 2 to 12 months of age); heifers (from 12 months of age until first calving); lactating cows; dry cows (non- lactating pregnant cows).

Emissions were estimated according to the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). A miscellaneous Tier approach was used for emission estimates: Tier 2 for the CH4 emissions from animal enteric fermentation and ma- nure handling, and Tier 1 for the direct and indirect N20 emissions (Table 3).

Primary and secondary contributions to GHG emissions for feed pur- chase, in farm feed production, energy & resources (other purchases), enteric fermentation, and manure handling are depicted in Fig. 1.

The CH4 emission from manure was calculated as a percentage of the excreted volatile solids (IPCC, 2006b). The estimate of direct N2O emission considered the amount of manure/slurry produced (m ³/year)

<list>- Energy & resources: type of energy, fuels, type of bedding materials and sanitizers;

Collected primary data were validated through consultation with the farmer manager and supported with additional information collected and related to breeding activities such as:

<list>- Planned manure agronomic use; 
- Fertilizers and pesticides register; 
- Amount of gasoline used (from the agricultural fuel register); 
- Energy consumption; 
- Collective synthetic breed (i.e., functional herd data); 
- Amount of feed purchase; 
- Milk composition; 
- Composition of diets fed to calves, young heifers, heifers, lactating cows, dry cows.</list>

To simplify data collection and estimate of the CF, animals were classified according to IPCC (2006) guidelines: calves (from birth to weaning); young heifers (from 2 to 12 months of age); heifers (from 12 months of age until first calving); lactating cows; dry cows (non- lactating pregnant cows).

Emissions were estimated according to the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). A miscellaneous Tier approach was used for emission estimates: Tier 2 for the CH4 emissions from animal enteric fermentation and ma- nure handling, and Tier 1 for the direct and indirect N20 emissions (Table 3).

Primary and secondary contributions to GHG emissions for feed pur- chase, in farm feed production, energy & resources (other purchases), enteric fermentation, and manure handling are depicted in Fig. 1.

The CH4 emission from manure was calculated as a percentage of the excreted volatile solids (IPCC, 2006b). The estimate of direct N2O emission considered the amount of manure/slurry produced (m ³/year)

13: <header>2.3. Description of the dairy production system and inventory data collection </header>

The surveyed farms were classified according to quartiles for the number of lactating dairy cows. Three groups were obtained: small herds (number of lactating dairy cows lower than the first quartile; < 87), medium herds (number of lactating dairy cows in between first and third quartile, 87 and 270), large herds (number of lactating dairy cows higher than the third quartile, > 270).

Table 2 reports averages of inventory data collected in dairy farms. Creating the life cycle inventory (LCI) allows understanding the input and output flow materials and the use of resources and energy within the system under study (Crawford et al., 2018). Animal breeding contributes to the emissions of CH4, N2O, and CO2 (Ammar et al., 2020) that can be ranked in primary (Finnegan et al., 2017) and secondary sources of emissions (Wilkes et al., 2020; Rotz et al., 2010). Primary emissions refer to the GHG emitted by the milk production phase, while secondary emissions are from sectors producing products and used by the system (i.e., dairy farming).

Information was grouped by feed purchase, in farm feed production, and energy & resources used and were reported as average for all herds and obtained groups based on the size of the lactating dairy cows herd. Data were collected through a checklist and referred to the following macro-areas:

<list>Breeding: number of animals and characteristics of the different cat- legories, purchase or sale of animals, performance data, phenotyping data; 
Milk production: milk yield and quality (fat and protein contents); 
Soil management for in farm crop production: cultivation, land and crop management, inputs used such as fertilizers, pesticides, and fuel; 
Feeds used in animal feeding: type, nutrients composition, origin (purchased or in farm produced);</list>

15: <header>2.3. Description of the dairy production system and inventory data collection </header>

The surveyed farms were classified according to quartiles for the number of lactating dairy cows. Three groups were obtained: small herds (number of lactating dairy cows lower than the first quartile; < 87), medium herds (number of lactating dairy cows in between first and third quartile, 87 and 270), large herds (number of lactating dairy cows higher than the third quartile, > 270).

Table 2 reports averages of inventory data collected in dairy farms. Creating the life cycle inventory (LCI) allows understanding the input and output flow materials and the use of resources and energy within the system under study (Crawford et al., 2018). Animal breeding contributes to the emissions of CH4, N2O, and CO2 (Ammar et al., 2020) that can be ranked in primary (Finnegan et al., 2017) and secondary sources of emissions (Wilkes et al., 2020; Rotz et al., 2010). Primary emissions refer to the GHG emitted by the milk production phase, while secondary emissions are from sectors producing products and used by the system (i.e., dairy farming).

Information was grouped by feed purchase, in farm feed production, and energy & resources used and were reported as average for all herds and obtained groups based on the size of the lactating dairy cows herd. Data were collected through a checklist and referred to the following macro-areas:

<list>Breeding: number of animals and characteristics of the different cat- legories, purchase or sale of animals, performance data, phenotyping data; 
Milk production: milk yield and quality (fat and protein contents); 
Soil management for in farm crop production: cultivation, land and crop management, inputs used such as fertilizers, pesticides, and fuel; 
Feeds used in animal feeding: type, nutrients composition, origin (purchased or in farm produced);</list>

Fig. 1. System boundary, input and output considered in the LCA study in the description of the dairy farm system.

<list>- Energy & resources: type of energy, fuels, type of bedding materials and sanitizers; 
- Manure management: type of manure and/or slurry production, stor- age and system used for on-field distribution.</list>
Collected primary data were validated through consultation with the farmer manager and supported with additional information collected and related to breeding activities such as:

<list>- Planned manure agronomic use; 
- Fertilizers and pesticides register; 
- Amount of gasoline used (from the agricultural fuel register); 
- Energy consumption; 
- Collective synthetic breed (i.e., functional herd data); 
- Amount of feed purchase; 
- Milk composition; 
- Composition of diets fed to calves, young heifers, heifers, lactating cows, dry cows.</list>

To simplify data collection and estimate of the CF, animals were classified according to IPCC (2006) guidelines: calves (from birth to weaning); young heifers (from 2 to 12 months of age); heifers (from 12 months of age until first calving); lactating cows; dry cows (non- lactating pregnant cows).

Emissions were estimated according to the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). A miscellaneous Tier approach was used for emission estimates: Tier 2 for the CH4 emissions from animal enteric fermentation and ma- nure handling, and Tier 1 for the direct and indirect N20 emissions (Table 3).

Primary and secondary contributions to GHG emissions for feed pur- chase, in farm feed production, energy & resources (other purchases), enteric fermentation, and manure handling are depicted in Fig. 1.

The CH4 emission from manure was calculated as a percentage of the excreted volatile solids (IPCC, 2006b). The estimate of direct N2O emission considered the amount of manure/slurry produced (m ³/year)

18: <header>2.3. Description of the dairy production system and inventory data collection </header>

The surveyed farms were classified according to quartiles for the number of lactating dairy cows. Three groups were obtained: small herds (number of lactating dairy cows lower than the first quartile; < 87), medium herds (number of lactating dairy cows in between first and third quartile, 87 and 270), large herds (number of lactating dairy cows higher than the third quartile, > 270).

Table 2 reports averages of inventory data collected in dairy farms. Creating the life cycle inventory (LCI) allows understanding the input and output flow materials and the use of resources and energy within the system under study (Crawford et al., 2018). Animal breeding contributes to the emissions of CH4, N2O, and CO2 (Ammar et al., 2020) that can be ranked in primary (Finnegan et al., 2017) and secondary sources of emissions (Wilkes et al., 2020; Rotz et al., 2010). Primary emissions refer to the GHG emitted by the milk production phase, while secondary emissions are from sectors producing products and used by the system (i.e., dairy farming).

Information was grouped by feed purchase, in farm feed production, and energy & resources used and were reported as average for all herds and obtained groups based on the size of the lactating dairy cows herd. Data were collected through a checklist and referred to the following macro-areas:

<list>Breeding: number of animals and characteristics of the different cat- legories, purchase or sale of animals, performance data, phenotyping data; 
Milk production: milk yield and quality (fat and protein contents); 
Soil management for in farm crop production: cultivation, land and crop management, inputs used such as fertilizers, pesticides, and fuel; 
Feeds used in animal feeding: type, nutrients composition, origin (purchased or in farm produced);</list>

Fig. 1. System boundary, input and output considered in the LCA study in the description of the dairy farm system.

<list>- Energy & resources: type of energy, fuels, type of bedding materials and sanitizers; 
- Manure management: type of manure and/or slurry production, stor- age and system used for on-field distribution.</list>
Collected primary data were validated through consultation with the farmer manager and supported with additional information collected and related to breeding activities such as:

<list>- Planned manure agronomic use; 
- Fertilizers and pesticides register; 
- Amount of gasoline used (from the agricultural fuel register); 
- Energy consumption; 
- Collective synthetic breed (i.e., functional herd data); 
- Amount of feed purchase; 
- Milk composition; 
- Composition of diets fed to calves, young heifers, heifers, lactating cows, dry cows.</list>

To simplify data collection and estimate of the CF, animals were classified according to IPCC (2006) guidelines: calves (from birth to weaning); young heifers (from 2 to 12 months of age); heifers (from 12 months of age until first calving); lactating cows; dry cows (non- lactating pregnant cows).

Emissions were estimated according to the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). A miscellaneous Tier approach was used for emission estimates: Tier 2 for the CH4 emissions from animal enteric fermentation and ma- nure handling, and Tier 1 for the direct and indirect N20 emissions (Table 3).

Primary and secondary contributions to GHG emissions for feed pur- chase, in farm feed production, energy & resources (other purchases), enteric fermentation, and manure handling are depicted in Fig. 1.

The CH4 emission from manure was calculated as a percentage of the excreted volatile solids (IPCC, 2006b). The estimate of direct N2O emission considered the amount of manure/slurry produced (m ³/year)

<list>- Energy & resources: type of energy, fuels, type of bedding materials and sanitizers;

Collected primary data were validated through consultation with the farmer manager and supported with additional information collected and related to breeding activities such as:

<list>- Planned manure agronomic use; 
- Fertilizers and pesticides register; 
- Amount of gasoline used (from the agricultural fuel register); 
- Energy consumption; 
- Collective synthetic breed (i.e., functional herd data); 
- Amount of feed purchase; 
- Milk composition; 
- Composition of diets fed to calves, young heifers, heifers, lactating cows, dry cows.</list>

To simplify data collection and estimate of the CF, animals were classified according to IPCC (2006) guidelines: calves (from birth to weaning); young heifers (from 2 to 12 months of age); heifers (from 12 months of age until first calving); lactating cows; dry cows (non- lactating pregnant cows).

Emissions were estimated according to the IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). A miscellaneous Tier approach was used for emission estimates: Tier 2 for the CH4 emissions from animal enteric fermentation and ma- nure handling, and Tier 1 for the direct and indirect N20 emissions (Table 3).

Primary and secondary contributions to GHG emissions for feed pur- chase, in farm feed production, energy & resources (other purchases), enteric fermentation, and manure handling are depicted in Fig. 1.

The CH4 emission from manure was calculated as a percentage of the excreted volatile solids (IPCC, 2006b). The estimate of direct N2O emission considered the amount of manure/slurry produced (m ³/year)

25: The SimaPro software v8.0.5. (PRé Consultants, 2015) was used for LCA analysis. The Ecoinvent v3.1 (Weidema et al., 2013) and Agri- Footprint v1.0 databases (Blonk Consultants, 2014) were used for pro- cessing primary and secondary data (Dalla Riva et al., 2105). The Ecoin- vent and Agri-footprint are globally recognized LCI databases. While the Ecoinvent database contains information related to different industrial sectors, the Agri-footprint specializes in the agri-food sector, particu- larly in production processes and agricultural products such as animal feed and food. The impact assessments were performed by SimaPro® based on the LCI dataset combined with processes present either in the Ecoinvent v3.1 or Agri-Footprint v1.0 databases. As previously adopted (Stocker et al., 2013), the global warming potential (GWP) was calcu- lated according to the IPCC method and over a time horizon of 100 years. The total GHG emissions were expressed as CO2-eq units using a GWP conversion of 1 kg of CO2-eq/kg CO2, 25 kg of CO2-eq/kg CH4, and 298 kg of CO2-eq/kg N2O (Signor and Cerri, 2013).
26: IBM SPSS Statistics v.26 (IBM Corp, 2019) was used to perform a MC simulation to investigate the variation of CF as related to the varia- tion of contributors (Table and how inputs contribute to the variance

of the outputs. Because the output of MC simulation is affected by the distribution function of the considered variables contributing to the tar- get of the simulation (CF), the assessment of proper parameters of the model fitting the original dataset from where samples are drawn is a crucial step in the MC simulation. Thus, the observed fitted distribution on original datasets for the itemized contribution to the feed purchase category, the in farm feed production, the energy & resources, the en- teric fermentation, and the manure handling categories were used in MC simulation. In addition, a 0.1 percent change of the 95% confidence interval of the distribution of the target's mean was chosen as the sim- ulation's endpoint.
27: <header>3.1. Characteristics of the farms considered for the study </header>

Main farm and herd characteristics are reported in Table 1, either as the average of all herds or for defined groups. The main breed in dairy herds was Friesian Holstein, whereas the Jersey breed was also present in some herds as spot lactating cows. None of the visited farms practiced grazing, and medium and large herds were kept for 12 months/year in free stalls in cubicles/bedded pack with or without the use of straw, while in small herds, 35.7% of lactating cows were kept in tied stall barn. Small herds were characterized by small size farms, on average 35 ha, and numerically lower FPCM yield/head (8069 kg/head) com- pared with medium (73 ha and 10,234 kg/head) and large herds (181 ha and 10,076 kg/head). As the farm size decreased, small herds seemed needier to the market for feed purchase, probably due to reduced avail- ability of human resources (i.e., family-run farms) and farm accessi- bility for equipment needed for crops growing (mainly for tillage). On the other hand, small herds were characterized by a numerically lower dairy cow replacement rate (21.8%) compared with medium (26.3%) and large (30.7%) herds. As a result, the average number of lactations was higher in small herds (3.7) than medium and large herds (3.1 and 2.8, respectively). Nonetheless, even if milk quality was similar among the three groups, the FPCM yield was, on average, lower in small herds,

The inventory data of produced in farm feeds are reported in Table 5. The land occupancy of homegrown crops is reported as the sum of the land area cultivated (ha) and as a percentage of the total area of the dairy farms size. Maize for silage and high moisture corn together accounted for 33, 39, and 30.1% of the cultivated area for small, medium, and large herds, respectively, followed in small and medium herds by alfalfa (32.9, 20.6% of the cultivated area) and ryegrass (6.7, 9.6% of the cultivated area). In large herds, the presence of wheat was higher (19.6% of the cultivated area), suggesting an increase of the grain in diet formulation. When considering all herds, maize (either silage or grain) was the main crop (42%), followed by alfalfa (18.5%, mainly used as sun-dry hay) and wheat (13.4%, mainly used as grain).

33: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts of the milk production systems </header>

The calculated CF at the farm gate was 1.19 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM (Table 4). Significant contributions were from enteric fermentation (43.3%), feed purchase (29.1%), and manure handling (14.6%). On the other hand, in farm feed production and energy & resources used had minor contributions accounting for 8.3% and 4.6% of the total CF, re- spectively.

The overall CF (kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) was in line with earlier stud- ies in the same geographical area (1.30 in Guerci et al. 2013; 1.18 in Battini et al. 2014); however, differences were observed in the CF shar- ing. Over a 41 dairy farms sample, Guerci et al. (2013) report 77% of GHG as in-farm produced and 50% from animal housing, enteric fer- mentation, manure storage and handling. The enteric fermentation and manure handling accounted for about 60% of CF in a typical North- ern Italian dairy farm (Battini et al., 2014). A higher contribution was observed in our condition, in which the enteric fermentation and the manure handling accounted for a total of 57.9% of the CF. The dif- ference might be explained by an underestimate of emission from en- teric fermentation in Guerci et al. (2013), where values were predicted based on dry matter intake and not calculated according to the IPCC (2006). Moreover, the same authors reported 21% emissions associ- ated to feed purchase and transport, suggesting a lower market need than what was observed in our condition (29.1%) without considering transport. Baldini et al. (2018) report 1.44 kg CO2-eq kg FPCM on three dairy cattle farms identifying enteric fermentation as the primary contributor followed by manure management, reporting higher values when applying direct measurements than the IPCC (2006). A similar value (1.43 kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM at the farm gate) was obtained by

Famiglietti et al. (2019) on a "cradle to distribution center gate" analy- sis involving three dairy farms. However, no information was provided about the different categories' contributions in data collection.

In contrast, our CF was lower than obtained in similar studies on farms located in Northern Italy. Dalla Riva et al. (2015), from a study on 34 dairy farms, reported 1.80 kg CO2-eq kg FPCM: 53% from feed purchase and 37% from enteric fermentation, the latter predicted on dry matter intake and not according to the IPCC (2006). The origin of feed (i.e., country) and the transport was underlined as critical for high CF (1.46 CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) of milk produced for cheese-making (Bava et al., 2018). The soybean meal from Latin American countries is characterized by a CF value of 6.77 kg of CO2-eq kg-1 In comparison, its Italian counterpart is about 154% lower (2.67 kg CO2-eq (Agri- Footprint v1.0). Alternative protein sources to extra EU soybean meal, not affected by LUC, could be lupin (1.47), pea protein (0.67), and alfalfa (0.43) (Mendowski et al., 2019). The cultivation of these crops could have several advantages like lower nitrogen fertilizer requirements and integration into low-input agricultural systems (Lehuger et al., 2009). However, the solution would imply changes in land management and crop growing, which is not always possible. Also, in our findings, the origin of proteic feeds (primary soybean) was a critical issue, with 19.5% of CF explained by the purchase of extra EU feeds (Table 4).

Recent work in areas other than the Po Valley reports CF values rang- ing from 1.25 to 1.32 kg CO2-eq FPCM (Romano et al., 2021), with about 40% contribution from methane and feed as found in our condi- tion. A regional and national study about environmental footprints of dairy farms in the United States (Rotz et al., 2021) reports emission in- tensities of 1.01 + 0.09 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM, which is in line with our average value of CF. Factors affecting the CF of milk are several: the specificity of the animal breeding (i.e., milk or meat) (Mazzetto et al., 2020), precision livestock farming (Berckmans, 2014), and animal health and welfare (Tullo et al., 2019). In addition, the proper manage- ment of the herd (i.e., age at first calving, heat stress abatement, dis- ease control, reproductive performance, genetics) reduced the enteric methane per kg of FPCM by 9 to 19% (Knapp et al., 2014).

In our condition, small herds had a higher (P < 0.05) CF (1.35 kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) compared with medium and large herds averaging 1.13 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM (Table 6). The higher CF value for small herds can be explained by the low milk yield (26.5 kg FPCM/head/day) over a 305 days lactation period and by the adopted feeding system. Even though diets fed to lactating cows were based on forages, silage feeds, and concentrate supplements as in medium and large herds, smaller herds were characterized by the presence of tied cows also, in which the feeding system was not based on a total mixed ration. As op-
Table 6 Carbon footprint (CF) expressed as kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM and group contributor for milk produced in different herd's size.

|Small herds< 87 lactating cows|Medium herds87 270 lactating cows|Large herds 270 lactating COWS|Large herds 270 lactating COWS |n 14|n 25|n 13|SE CF|1.35E+00|1.14E+00b|1.11E+00b|5.45E-02 1 Feed purchase|4.09E-01|3.38E-01|2.94E-01|4.06E-02 In farm feed production|1.24E-01|9.12E-02b|8.43E-02b|7.69E-03 Energy & resources|7.07E-02|5.08E-02b|4.42E-02b|5.95E-03 Enteric fermentation|5.65E-01a|4.91E-01b|5.06E-01|1.73E-02 1 Manure handling|1.78E-01|1.67E-01|1.82E-01|1.05E-02

posed to total mixed rations, traditional feeding systems result in higher CF estimates (Schingoethe, 2017). In our condition, the presence of tied cows, the numeric differences in replacement rate, and the number of lactations were ultimately confounding factors that might impact milk yield and, therefore, the calculated CF for the functional unit.

In particular, small herds had higher CF (P < 0.05) from enteric fer- mentation, in farm feed production, energy & resources used, and feed purchase compared with medium (only for the former) and large herds. The reliance of small herds on protein feeds from extra EU could be explained by the need to seek economic advantages in a condition char- acterized by the low-profit margin due to the low milk yield. Also, the observed lower energy efficiency could be due to the lack of heat recov- ery systems on milk tanks or optimization systems for cleaning agents. In addition, the higher CF due to the in farm feed production observed in small herds could be explained by higher use of synthetic fertilizers (0.26 ton/ha VS. 0.21 ton/ha in large herds) rather than valuing live- stock manure as support for homegrown crops, mainly because of lack in manure distribution equipment.

The dif- ference might be explained by an underestimate of emission from en- teric fermentation in Guerci et al. (2013), where values were predicted based on dry matter intake and not calculated according to the IPCC (2006). Moreover, the same authors reported 21% emissions associ- ated to feed purchase and transport, suggesting a lower market need than what was observed in our condition (29.1%) without considering transport. Baldini et al. (2018) report 1.44 kg CO2-eq kg FPCM on three dairy cattle farms identifying enteric fermentation as the primary contributor followed by manure management, reporting higher values when applying direct measurements than the IPCC (2006). A similar value (1.43 kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM at the farm gate) was obtained by

Famiglietti et al. (2019) on a "cradle to distribution center gate" analy- sis involving three dairy farms. However, no information was provided about the different categories' contributions in data collection.

In contrast, our CF was lower than obtained in similar studies on farms located in Northern Italy. Dalla Riva et al. (2015), from a study on 34 dairy farms, reported 1.80 kg CO2-eq kg FPCM: 53% from feed purchase and 37% from enteric fermentation, the latter predicted on dry matter intake and not according to the IPCC (2006). The origin of feed (i.e., country) and the transport was underlined as critical for high CF (1.46 CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) of milk produced for cheese-making (Bava et al., 2018). The soybean meal from Latin American countries is characterized by a CF value of 6.77 kg of CO2-eq kg-1 In comparison, its Italian counterpart is about 154% lower (2.67 kg CO2-eq (Agri- Footprint v1.0). Alternative protein sources to extra EU soybean meal, not affected by LUC, could be lupin (1.47), pea protein (0.67), and alfalfa (0.43) (Mendowski et al., 2019). The cultivation of these crops could have several advantages like lower nitrogen fertilizer requirements and integration into low-input agricultural systems (Lehuger et al., 2009). However, the solution would imply changes in land management and crop growing, which is not always possible. Also, in our findings, the origin of proteic feeds (primary soybean) was a critical issue, with 19.5% of CF explained by the purchase of extra EU feeds (Table 4).

Recent work in areas other than the Po Valley reports CF values rang- ing from 1.25 to 1.32 kg CO2-eq FPCM (Romano et al., 2021), with about 40% contribution from methane and feed as found in our condi- tion. A regional and national study about environmental footprints of dairy farms in the United States (Rotz et al., 2021) reports emission in- tensities of 1.01 + 0.09 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM, which is in line with our average value of CF. Factors affecting the CF of milk are several: the specificity of the animal breeding (i.e., milk or meat) (Mazzetto et al., 2020), precision livestock farming (Berckmans, 2014), and animal health and welfare (Tullo et al., 2019). In addition, the proper manage- ment of the herd (i.e., age at first calving, heat stress abatement, dis- ease control, reproductive performance, genetics) reduced the enteric methane per kg of FPCM by 9 to 19% (Knapp et al., 2014).

In our condition, small herds had a higher (P < 0.05) CF (1.35 kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) compared with medium and large herds averaging 1.13 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM (Table 6). The higher CF value for small herds can be explained by the low milk yield (26.5 kg FPCM/head/day) over a 305 days lactation period and by the adopted feeding system. Even though diets fed to lactating cows were based on forages, silage feeds, and concentrate supplements as in medium and large herds, smaller herds were characterized by the presence of tied cows also, in which the feeding system was not based on a total mixed ration. As op-

posed to total mixed rations, traditional feeding systems result in higher CF estimates (Schingoethe, 2017). In particular, small herds had higher CF (P < 0.05) from enteric fer- mentation, in farm feed production, energy & resources used, and feed purchase compared with medium (only for the former) and large herds. The reliance of small herds on protein feeds from extra EU could be explained by the need to seek economic advantages in a condition char- acterized by the low-profit margin due to the low milk yield. Also, the observed lower energy efficiency could be due to the lack of heat recov- ery systems on milk tanks or optimization systems for cleaning agents. In addition, the higher CF due to the in farm feed production observed in small herds could be explained by higher use of synthetic fertilizers (0.26 ton/ha VS. 0.21 ton/ha in large herds) rather than valuing live- stock manure as support for homegrown crops, mainly because of lack in manure distribution equipment.

The calculated CF at the farm gate was 1.19 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM (Table 4). Significant contributions were from enteric fermentation (43.3%), feed purchase (29.1%), and manure handling (14.6%). Factors affecting the CF of milk are several: the specificity of the animal breeding (i.e., milk or meat) (Mazzetto et al., 2020), precision livestock farming (Berckmans, 2014), and animal health and welfare (Tullo et al., 2019). In addition, the proper manage- ment of the herd (i.e., age at first calving, heat stress abatement, dis- ease control, reproductive performance, genetics) reduced the enteric methane per kg of FPCM by 9 to 19% (Knapp et al., 2014).

In our condition, small herds had a higher (P < 0.05) CF (1.35 kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) compared with medium and large herds averaging 1.13 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM (Table 6). The higher CF value for small herds can be explained by the low milk yield (26.5 kg FPCM/head/day) over a 305 days lactation period and by the adopted feeding system. Even though diets fed to lactating cows were based on forages, silage feeds, and concentrate supplements as in medium and large herds, smaller herds were characterized by the presence of tied cows also, in which the feeding system was not based on a total mixed ration. In particular, small herds had higher CF (P < 0.05) from enteric fer- mentation, in farm feed production, energy & resources used, and feed purchase compared with medium (only for the former) and large herds. The reliance of small herds on protein feeds from extra EU could be explained by the need to seek economic advantages in a condition char- acterized by the low-profit margin due to the low milk yield. Also, the observed lower energy efficiency could be due to the lack of heat recov- ery systems on milk tanks or optimization systems for cleaning agents. In addition, the higher CF due to the in farm feed production observed in small herds could be explained by higher use of synthetic fertilizers (0.26 ton/ha VS. 0.21 ton/ha in large herds) rather than valuing live- stock manure as support for homegrown crops, mainly because of lack in manure distribution equipment.
39: <header>3.2. Environmental impacts of the milk production systems </header>

The calculated CF at the farm gate was 1.19 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM (Table 4). Significant contributions were from enteric fermentation (43.3%), feed purchase (29.1%), and manure handling (14.6%). On the other hand, in farm feed production and energy & resources used had minor contributions accounting for 8.3% and 4.6% of the total CF, re- spectively.

The overall CF (kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) was in line with earlier stud- ies in the same geographical area (1.30 in Guerci et al. 2013; 1.18 in Battini et al. 2014); however, differences were observed in the CF shar- ing. Over a 41 dairy farms sample, Guerci et al. (2013) report 77% of GHG as in-farm produced and 50% from animal housing, enteric fer- mentation, manure storage and handling. The enteric fermentation and manure handling accounted for about 60% of CF in a typical North- ern Italian dairy farm (Battini et al., 2014). A higher contribution was observed in our condition, in which the enteric fermentation and the manure handling accounted for a total of 57.9% of the CF. The dif- ference might be explained by an underestimate of emission from en- teric fermentation in Guerci et al. (2013), where values were predicted based on dry matter intake and not calculated according to the IPCC (2006). Moreover, the same authors reported 21% emissions associ- ated to feed purchase and transport, suggesting a lower market need than what was observed in our condition (29.1%) without considering transport. Baldini et al. (2018) report 1.44 kg CO2-eq kg FPCM on three dairy cattle farms identifying enteric fermentation as the primary contributor followed by manure management, reporting higher values when applying direct measurements than the IPCC (2006). A similar value (1.43 kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM at the farm gate) was obtained by

Famiglietti et al. (2019) on a "cradle to distribution center gate" analy- sis involving three dairy farms. However, no information was provided about the different categories' contributions in data collection.

In contrast, our CF was lower than obtained in similar studies on farms located in Northern Italy. Dalla Riva et al. (2015), from a study on 34 dairy farms, reported 1.80 kg CO2-eq kg FPCM: 53% from feed purchase and 37% from enteric fermentation, the latter predicted on dry matter intake and not according to the IPCC (2006). The origin of feed (i.e., country) and the transport was underlined as critical for high CF (1.46 CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) of milk produced for cheese-making (Bava et al., 2018). The soybean meal from Latin American countries is characterized by a CF value of 6.77 kg of CO2-eq kg-1 In comparison, its Italian counterpart is about 154% lower (2.67 kg CO2-eq (Agri- Footprint v1.0). Alternative protein sources to extra EU soybean meal, not affected by LUC, could be lupin (1.47), pea protein (0.67), and alfalfa (0.43) (Mendowski et al., 2019). The cultivation of these crops could have several advantages like lower nitrogen fertilizer requirements and integration into low-input agricultural systems (Lehuger et al., 2009). However, the solution would imply changes in land management and crop growing, which is not always possible. Also, in our findings, the origin of proteic feeds (primary soybean) was a critical issue, with 19.5% of CF explained by the purchase of extra EU feeds (Table 4).

Recent work in areas other than the Po Valley reports CF values rang- ing from 1.25 to 1.32 kg CO2-eq FPCM (Romano et al., 2021), with about 40% contribution from methane and feed as found in our condi- tion. A regional and national study about environmental footprints of dairy farms in the United States (Rotz et al., 2021) reports emission in- tensities of 1.01 + 0.09 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM, which is in line with our average value of CF. Factors affecting the CF of milk are several: the specificity of the animal breeding (i.e., milk or meat) (Mazzetto et al., 2020), precision livestock farming (Berckmans, 2014), and animal health and welfare (Tullo et al., 2019). In addition, the proper manage- ment of the herd (i.e., age at first calving, heat stress abatement, dis- ease control, reproductive performance, genetics) reduced the enteric methane per kg of FPCM by 9 to 19% (Knapp et al., 2014).

In our condition, small herds had a higher (P < 0.05) CF (1.35 kg CO2-eq kg-1 FPCM) compared with medium and large herds averaging 1.13 kg CO2-eq kg- FPCM (Table 6). The higher CF value for small herds can be explained by the low milk yield (26.5 kg FPCM/head/day) over a 305 days lactation period and by the adopted feeding system. Even though diets fed to lactating cows were based on forages, silage feeds, and concentrate supplements as in medium and large herds, smaller herds were characterized by the presence of tied cows also, in which the feeding system was not based on a total mixed ration. 41: Adopting a mitigation solution following an LCA study must rely on proper data collection and output interpretation. Because there can be uncertainties and variations in the data used (Sonnemann et al., 2002), the assessment of the quality of the data, and therefore uncertainty as- sociated with them, are critical aspects in communicating results on an LCA assessment (Weidema, 2000). Even though the uncertainty analy- sis can be carried out by software used in the LCA analysis, limitations might prompt from the few available fitting distributions, the required knowledge of parameters needed to run the analysis, and the analysis confined to a single record (i.e., the farm). In addition, because each dairy farm is unique in terms of available land, feeds, and housing fa- cilities affecting herd management, and because several interactions are present among processes within and between considered categories, the output of sensitivity analysis carried out on a single farm could be er- roneous and misleading, and the solution of using average inputs for considered categories will not represent the average farm. Therefore, we modeled the CF in each considered category and investigated how the category variation was affecting the variation of CF for the FU.

The MC simulation was carried out over all herds, regardless of herd size. Fig. 2 reports the effect of one unit change in the standard devia- tion of considered categories on the estimate of the CF. The feed pur- chase had the higher (13.4%) impact on CF change, followed by enteric fermentation (5.9%), manure handling (3.4%), in farm feed production (2.5%), and energy & resources used (1.7%). The contribution of vari- ance (Fig. 3) was higher for feed purchase (63.9%) followed by enteric fermentation (19%) and manure (10.2%), suggesting variability of feed purchase could affect the CF value, also supported by the observed cor- relation between CF of categories and CF of the FU (Fig. 4). When feed purchase was itemized, in our condition, the most remarkable change on CF from changes in the standard deviation of contributors was from ex- tra EU feeds, 10.9% and 8.4%, respectively for concentrate and proteic feeds (Fig. 5). It is essential to remind that soybean meal from South
Fig. 2. Change in CF for variation of +/-1 standard deviation for the considered categories of input (value in kg CO2 eq kg- FPCM).
Fig. 3. Contribution to variance (value in%) for variation of +/- 1 standard deviation of considered input categories.
Fig. 4. Correlation between input values and target of the model (CF).

America was included among feeds used in concentrates formulation. The extra EU soybean was also included, as by-products of soybean pro- cessing, in proteic feeds used in animal feeding. However, the contribu- tion to the variance of the latter was higher (23.2%) compared to a more standardized composition of extra EU concentrates (7%). In contrast, ce-
Fig. Change in CF for variation of +/-1 standard deviation for the considered categories of inputs (value in kg CO2 eq kg-1 FPCM), with reference to the breakdown of the feed purchase category.
Fig. 6. Contribution to variance (value in%) for variation of +/- 1 standard deviation of considered input categories.

reals with a value of 16.2% were the second category of purchased feeds, affecting the variance (Fig. 6).

The concentrate EU and proteic EU feeds were negatively correlated when looking at the correlation of the considered categories and CF of the FU. However, due to LUC, positive and higher correlations were observed for the same categories considering extra EU feeds (Fig. 7). Among other considered categories, enteric fermentation and the in farm feed production had the highest positive correlation with CF of the FU.

45: Adopting a mitigation solution following an LCA study must rely on proper data collection and output interpretation. Because there can be uncertainties and variations in the data used (Sonnemann et al., 2002), the assessment of the quality of the data, and therefore uncertainty as- sociated with them, are critical aspects in communicating results on an LCA assessment (Weidema, 2000). Even though the uncertainty analy- sis can be carried out by software used in the LCA analysis, limitations might prompt from the few available fitting distributions, the required knowledge of parameters needed to run the analysis, and the analysis confined to a single record (i.e., the farm). In addition, because each dairy farm is unique in terms of available land, feeds, and housing fa- cilities affecting herd management, and because several interactions are present among processes within and between considered categories, the output of sensitivity analysis carried out on a single farm could be er- roneous and misleading, and the solution of using average inputs for considered categories will not represent the average farm. Therefore, we modeled the CF in each considered category and investigated how the category variation was affecting the variation of CF for the FU.

The MC simulation was carried out over all herds, regardless of herd size. Fig. 2 reports the effect of one unit change in the standard devia- tion of considered categories on the estimate of the CF. The feed pur- chase had the higher (13.4%) impact on CF change, followed by enteric fermentation (5.9%), manure handling (3.4%), in farm feed production (2.5%), and energy & resources used (1.7%). The contribution of vari- ance (Fig. 3) was higher for feed purchase (63.9%) followed by enteric fermentation (19%) and manure (10.2%), suggesting variability of feed purchase could affect the CF value, also supported by the observed cor- relation between CF of categories and CF of the FU (Fig. 4). When feed purchase was itemized, in our condition, the most remarkable change on CF from changes in the standard deviation of contributors was from ex- tra EU feeds, 10.9% and 8.4%, respectively for concentrate and proteic feeds (Fig. 5). It is essential to remind that soybean meal from South

Fig. 4. Correlation between input values and target of the model (CF).

America was included among feeds used in concentrates formulation. The extra EU soybean was also included, as by-products of soybean pro- cessing, in proteic feeds used in animal feeding. However, the contribu- tion to the variance of the latter was higher (23.2%) compared to a more standardized composition of extra EU concentrates (7%). In contrast, ce-

reals with a value of 16.2% were the second category of purchased feeds, affecting the variance (Fig. 6).

The concentrate EU and proteic EU feeds were negatively correlated when looking at the correlation of the considered categories and CF of the FU. However, due to LUC, positive and higher correlations were observed for the same categories considering extra EU feeds (Fig. 7). Among other considered categories, enteric fermentation and the in farm feed production had the highest positive correlation with CF of the FU.
46: The present work proposed an LCA analysis of raw milk produced in Northern Italy.
Results confirmed previous studies focusing on CF for a unit of milk at the farm gate. The main contributions were enteric fermentation, feed purchase, and manure handling.

The MC simulation showed the high variability on CF brought from feed purchase, with proteic extra EU and cereals feeds as primary con- tributors to the variance. The size of the herd affected the emission of GHG with high CF for the unit of milk produced in small herds. The low milk yield and the higher reliance on extra EU proteic feeds fostered the observed difference. A 16% improvement of CF could be expected if applying the management solution used in medium and large herds. The knowledge of contributors to CF should be foreseen for a mit- igation action to improve the environmental performance of milk pro- duction for human consumption. Furthermore, it should represent an opportunity for dairies to adopt corporate policies aimed at the envi- ronmental labeling of fresh milk.
